summary:
Ohalo II is a submerged prehistoric site on the southwestern shore of the Sea of Galilee in modern Israel, dated to approximately 23,000 BCE but with later occupation phases extending into the early Holocene around 9000–7500 BCE. The site is exceptionally well preserved due to submersion under lake sediments, retaining organic remains including charred plant material, wooden hut foundations, and fish bones. It provides unusually detailed evidence of pre-agricultural subsistence strategies in the Levant.

The plant remains from Ohalo II include wild cereals, legumes, and fruits, demonstrating that the communities who occupied the site had sophisticated knowledge of local plant resources. Evidence of a grinding stone with starch residues from multiple grain species suggests that some degree of plant processing was already established before formal cultivation began. Ohalo II is therefore important as a window onto the period immediately preceding the Neolithic transition, illustrating the intensive plant use strategies that preceded and likely paved the way for deliberate agriculture.

images.caption: Composite Sickles for Cereal Harvesting at 23,000-Years-Old Ohalo II, Israel....
